preFix.title: Throwing a sake cup set
preFix.subtitle: Six small wheel-thrown cups in stoneware with a chawan-influenced form
preFix.excerpt: Six small sake cups thrown from 100 g of stoneware each, with a slightly flared rim and a narrow foot ring. The tutorial covers throwing the small-cup form at speed using off-the-hump technique and a consistent rim diameter. Requires a potter's wheel and kiln access to cone 6.
preFix.sourceNotes: Sake cup form derived from first principles. V&A open-access collection for chawan and Japanese ceramic cup reference.

content.text: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard. Silicosis is irreversible. Mix wet whenever possible. Wear a P100 respirator any time raw glaze powders are open. Wet-clean only: no sweeping, no compressed air, no dry sanding indoors.
Ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor. Never open a hot kiln below 80 degrees C. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio.
Six sake cups from a single session. The
off the hump
technique makes this run efficient: 600 g of clay is centred once and six cups thrown from the top in sequence. The chawan-influenced form is wide at the rim relative to its height and has a narrow
foot ring
that emphasises the cup's base.

items.name: Smooth stoneware
items.quantity: 600 g (for 6 cups at approximately 100 g each)

content.text: Centring the hump
Attach all 600 g to the wheel head and centre precisely.
Centring
must be exact before throwing begins: each cup inherits any wobble from the hump. Once centred, draw up the top 100 g into a column for the first cup.
Throwing the cup form

Open the top of the column to 40 mm diameter and 40 mm deep. Pull the wall upward in two pulls to 55-60 mm tall. On the final pull, angle the inside fingers outward to flare the rim to about 75-80 mm diameter. The sake cup form should be wider at the rim than it is at the base: think of a shallow cone rather than a cylinder. Press the outside wall with the wooden rib to compress and refine the curve.
Cutting free

Level the base of the cup where it meets the hump. Hold the wire taut and run it flat across the top of the hump to cut the cup free. Slide the cup onto a board to dry. Re-wet the hump and draw up the next column. Repeat until all six cups are thrown.
Trimming and firing
At leather-hard, trim a narrow foot ring on each cup: 4-5 mm wide and 6-8 mm tall, set 8 mm in from the base edge. The narrow foot ring is characteristic of this cup style. Allow to reach bone-dry.
Bisque
at
cone
06. Apply a thin liner glaze on the interior before a full exterior glaze. Fire to cone 6.
Variations
Throw a matching serving carafe from 400 g of clay with a narrow neck for the full sake service.
Apply a wax-resist stripe around the outside before dipping in glaze for a two-tone effect.
Use a dark iron or tenmoku glaze on the exterior for a classic finish.

rows.fix: Measure column height with a needle tool against a fixed gauge before each throw. A consistent starting height gives consistent finished height.
rows.cause: Column heights were inconsistent before opening each cup
rows.problem: Cups vary in height across the set

rows.fix: On the next cup, apply outward pressure from inside fingers during the final upward pull more deliberately.
rows.cause: Insufficient outward flare on the final pull

rows.fix: Hold the wire taut at both ends and press it flat against the top of the hump surface before drawing inward.
rows.cause: Wire was angled rather than held flat
rows.problem: Wire cut goes through the base of the cup

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
